name: Object Particle (을/를)
summary: Mark the direct object of a verb with 을 or 를, the particle that says what the action is done to.
explanation:
The object particle 을/를 attaches to the noun that receives the action of a verb. In 'I eat an apple', the apple is the object, so it takes 을/를: 사과를 먹어요 (sagwareul meogeoyo).

The choice follows the final sound of the noun. Use 을 after a consonant and 를 after a vowel: 밥을 (babeul, rice), 우유를 (uyureul, milk). It works exactly like the topic and subject particles, just for objects.

Korean word order is subject-object-verb, so the object plus 을/를 sits right before the verb: 저는 한국어를 공부해요 (I study Korean). In casual speech the particle is often dropped, but keep it while learning so the sentence structure stays clear.
commonMistakes: Using 를 after a consonant or 을 after a vowel. The form depends on the final sound: 밥을 but 우유를.
Putting the object after the verb. Korean is subject-object-verb, so the object comes first: 밥을 먹어요.
Confusing 을/를 (object) with 이/가 (subject). The object receives the action; the subject performs it.
Adding 을/를 to a noun used with 이다 (to be). 'I am a student' uses 이에요, not an object particle.
Forgetting that some verbs like 만나다 (to meet) take an object in Korean even when English uses 'with'.
tip: Spot the object by asking 'what?' after the verb: eat what? rice. That noun takes 을/를.
